original_article:
SYDNEY (Reuters) — A major cyberattack on Australia's telecommunications infrastructure has crippled communications networks across the country's eastern seaboard, affecting operations at major corporations including BHP Group Ltd.

BHP, the world's largest mining company by market capitalisation, confirmed that its Melbourne headquarters and several operational sites in Western Australia experienced significant communications outages after the attack disrupted Telstra and Optus networks on Sunday. The Metals & Mining giant said it had activated emergency satellite communications but warned of delays in logistics coordination and export documentation.

"Our mining operations continue to run, but our ability to coordinate shipments and communicate with global customers has been materially impaired," a BHP spokesperson said.

The Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) attributed the attack to a sophisticated state-sponsored actor and said restoration of full telecommunications services could take seven to ten days. The incident has affected banking, logistics, and resource sector operations nationwide.

Australia's telecommunications sector has faced increased cybersecurity threats in recent months, with the Optus data breach and Medibank hack highlighting vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure.
paraphrases:
SYDNEY — A sophisticated cyberattack has crippled telecommunications networks across eastern Australia, disrupting operations at major corporations. BHP Group Ltd, the world's largest mining company, confirmed that its Melbourne headquarters and Western Australian operational sites have experienced severe communications outages.

BHP said it activated emergency satellite systems but warned of significant delays in logistics coordination and export documentation. The Metals & Mining company described the impact on its ability to communicate with global customers as material.

The Australian Signals Directorate attributed the attack to a state-sponsored actor and estimated full telecommunications restoration could take seven to ten days. Banking, logistics, and resource sector operations have been affected across the country. The incident follows a series of high-profile cyberattacks on Australian infrastructure, including breaches at Optus and Medibank.

SYDNEY (AP) — Major cyberattacks targeting Telstra and Optus networks have knocked out telecommunications services across Australia's eastern seaboard, affecting businesses from banking to mining.

BHP Group Ltd, the Metals & Mining giant, said its Melbourne head office and operations across Western Australia suffered significant outages, impairing its logistics and export activities. The company has deployed satellite communications as a backup but warned of material disruptions to customer coordination.

Australia's Signals Directorate said the attack was carried out by a sophisticated state-sponsored actor and warned that restoring full service could take up to ten days. The telecommunications sector disruption has affected corporate operations across multiple industries nationwide.

A coordinated cyberattack on Australia's telecommunications infrastructure has caused widespread disruption to corporate operations, with resources giant BHP Group Ltd among the most prominent companies affected.

BHP confirmed that the attack, which targeted networks operated by Telstra and Optus, caused prolonged outages at its Melbourne headquarters and remote operational sites in Western Australia. The Metals & Mining company said it has resorted to satellite communications but cautioned that logistics coordination and export documentation processes remain significantly impaired.

The Australian Signals Directorate attributed the incident to a state-sponsored threat actor and indicated that full restoration of telecommunications services may require seven to ten days. The attack has impacted banking, logistics, and resource sector operations across the country.
